day_trips_wine.name: Cognac, Charente
day_trips_wine.description: The Charente's Cognac town and its surrounding Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne crus, home to the great Cognac houses (Hennessy, Remy Martin, Prunier) and smaller grower distillers. A full-day trip from Bordeaux covers two house visits with Charentais pot-still tours and an aged XO tasting.
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: Maison Hennessy visitor centre is the best-organised and most multilingual; book the enhanced 'Paradis' tasting experience in advance.

day_trips_wine.name: Bergerac, Dordogne
day_trips_wine.description: The Bergerac wine region in the Dordogne valley, immediately east of Bordeaux, produces red and white wines from the same grape varieties: Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon. The Monbazillac appellation produces botrytised dessert wines that are stylistically similar to Sauternes at far lower prices.
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: Chateau Monbazillac (12th-century estate) is the most visitor-friendly producer with daily tours. Bergerac town centre has a compact wine quarter with tasting rooms.

day_trips_wine.name: Armagnac, Gers
day_trips_wine.description: Gascony's Bas Armagnac appellation in the Gers produces France's oldest brandy, distilled in the continuous alembic armagnacais style rather than Cognac's double-distillation. Single-vintage Armagnacs dating back to the 1920s are available from estate distillers including Domaine de Menard and Domaine d'Ognoas.
signature_pours: Domaine de Menard Bas Armagnac Vintage 2000
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: Condom is the main Armagnac town with a museum (Musee de l'Armagnac); the Aire-sur-l'Adour and Gondrin areas offer the densest concentration of estate distilleries open for visits.

day_trips_wine.name: Cahors, Lot (Malbec Country)
day_trips_wine.description: Cahors, the ancestral home of Malbec before phylloxera devastated it in Bordeaux, now produces some of France's most powerful and age-worthy red wines from Malbec (locally called Cot). The Lot river valley terroir produces structured, tannic, mineral expressions very different from Argentina's version.
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: The village of Cahors is UNESCO-listed and compact; two estates (Lagrezette and du Cedre) are within easy driving distance and both have excellent visitor facilities.

day_trips_wine.name: Madiran, Pyrenees-Atlantiques
day_trips_wine.description: Madiran in the Adour basin produces some of France's most tannic and age-worthy reds from Tannat, the iron-rich grape associated with Gascon cuisine. Chateau Bouscasse (Patrick Ducournau, inventor of micro-oxygenation) and Chateau Montus are the defining producers. Often paired with Pacherenc du Vic-Bilh (white) from the same zone.
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: Chateau Bouscasse (Alain Brumont) offers some of France's best estate restaurant lunches; book well in advance for the cellar tour and tasting menu pairing.

day_trips_wine.name: Saumur-Champigny, Loire Valley
day_trips_wine.description: A longer day-trip option: Saumur-Champigny is the Loire's great Cabernet Franc heartland, producing lighter-bodied, more aromatic expressions of the same grape that anchors Saint-Emilion's Right Bank. Domaine Filliatreau, Chateau Hureau, and Domaine des Roches Neuves are the reference producers. Contrasting the Loire style against Bordeaux Cabernet Franc is valuable for any serious wine student.
day_trips_wine.visitor_tip: Best combined with a night in Saumur; the Loire Valley's troglodyte cellar network (caves tuffeau) is the architectural counterpart to Bordeaux's limestone chais. Saumur city's museum of wine is compact and informative.
